title-group.article-title: PRRSV subverts host programmed cell death network for immune evasion

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome virus (PRRSV) is a major pathogen endangering the global swine industry, causing severe reproductive disorders in sows and respiratory diseases in piglets, and incurring substantial economic losses. This review summarizes how PRRSV interferes with multiple programmed cell death (PCD) modalities, including apoptosis, autophagy, pyroptosis, and ferroptosis, thereby optimizing its replication microenvironment, evading host immune responses, and establishing persistent infection. Furthermore, it details the spatiotemporal-specific strategies of PRRSV in regulating PCD during different infection stages and in various cell types, and highlights the pivotal role of virus-host protein interactions in viral pathogenesis. Collectively, this review systematically elucidates the molecular mechanisms by which PRRSV manipulates the host PCD network, and provides crucial theoretical guidance for PRRSV control research and antiviral drug development.

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome virus (PRRSV) is a single-stranded positive-sense RNA virus with a genome of approximately 15 kb in length, belonging to the genus Arterivirus within the family Arteriviridae. Its genome contains at least 11 open reading frames (ORFs), among which ORF1a and ORF1b encode replicase polyproteins that are cleaved to produce at least 14 non-structural proteins (NSP1α/β, NSP2–12), while ORFs 2–7 encode structural proteins (GP2–GP5, E, M, and N) [1,2]. In particular, the NSP2 gene, as the most variable region of the genome, encodes a protein comprising four structural domains (the PL2 protease domain, hypervariable region, transmembrane domain, and C-terminal conserved region), which plays a critical role in viral replication, virulence modulation, and immune evasion. remains one of the most economically devastating pathogens in global swine husbandry [3,4]. Since its emergence in the late 1980s, PRRSV has caused persistent reproductive failure in sows and severe respiratory distress in piglets, resulting in annual losses exceeding billions of dollars worldwide [5–7]. PRRSV exhibits high genetic diversity and antigenic variability, with two major genotypes: PRRSV-1 (European type) and PRRSV-2 (North American type), sharing only 50%-70% nucleotide identity [8]. In recent years, the epidemic strains in China have become increasingly diverse, including highly pathogenic PRRSV (HP-PRRSV), NADC30-like, and NADC34-like strains. Currently used modified live vaccines and inactivated vaccines offer limited protection due to the high variability of the virus. While homotypic protection is relatively effective, heterotypic protection remains poor, and risks such as virulence reversion, recombination, and antibody-dependent enhancement persist.

Understanding the pathogenesis of PRRSV is crucial for the development of novel antiviral interventions. Antiviral strategies targeting the PRRSV life cycle are being actively explored, including inhibitors that block viral entry, replication, and assembly (e.g. tylvalosin), as well as novel immune-based approaches such as broadly neutralizing antibodies, mRNA vaccines, and nanoparticle vaccines [9]. Additionally, various viral antagonists play distinct roles in the virus life cycle: NSP1α and NSP1β function as interferon antagonists to suppress host innate immunity; NSP2 is involved in immune modulation and is associated with virulence; NSP4 acts as the main protease and can induce apoptosis; and NSP11 inhibits interferon production and modulates cell death pathways through its endonuclease activity. A key aspect of its pathogenesis involves the modulation of host cell death pathways, which facilitates viral replication, immune evasion, and the establishment of persistent infection. Programmed cell death (PCD) represents a fundamental cellular process, encompassing apoptosis, autophagy, pyroptosis, ferroptosis, and necroptosis, that have evolutionarily served as a host defense mechanism to restrict viral replication and clear infected cells [10–14]. Nevertheless, PRRSV intricately manipulates multiple PCD pathways to foster a favorable intracellular environment for its replication and counteract antiviral immunity.

This review aims to systematically summarize current knowledge on how PRRSV fine-tunes these PCD mechanisms. By elucidating the complex interactions between PRRSV and host PCD pathways, we seek to provide insights into viral persistence and immune escape strategies, thereby highlighting potential targets for the development of next-generation therapeutics against porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome.

sec.title: Viral mechanisms of apoptosis regulation
sec.p: Apoptosis is a genetically controlled, programmed form of cell death characterized by typical morphological features such as cell shrinkage, chromatin condensation, and the formation of apoptotic bodies [15–17]. Depending on the origin of the initiating signals, the major pathways of apoptosis are classified into the extrinsic death receptor pathway, the intrinsic mitochondrial pathway, and the endoplasmic reticulum (ER) stress pathway [18–21]. Although activated by distinct upstream signals, these pathways ultimately converge on the caspase cascade, leading to the programmed dismantling of the cell through the ordered hydrolysis of key intracellular proteins. PRRSV effectively exploits apoptosis to facilitate viral replication and release. Studies have shown that various PRRSV genotypes, including the genotype 1 strain GZ11-G1 and the genotype 2 strains SD16, VR2332, and CH-1a, can induce apoptosis in host cells [22]. These PRRSV strains trigger G2/M phase arrest in host cells by activating the Chk/Cdc25C and p53/p21 signaling pathways. This arrest is manifested by reduced activity of the Cdc2-cyclinB1 complex, phosphorylation of Cdc2 at Tyr15, its nuclear accumulation, and tne concurrent inhibition of the complex by p21. The dysregulation of these key cell cycle regulators ultimately initiates the apoptosis process. This outcome not only suppresses host cell proliferation but also creates favorable conditions for viral replication [22]. This regulatory mechanism is highly conserved in both PRRSV genotypes 1 and 2, suggesting that, despite genetic differences, interfering with the host cell cycle to promote its own replication is a core strategy evolved by PRRSV. By actively manipulating the cell cycle, the virus replicates within infected cells and subsequently induces apoptosis to facilitate the release and spread of viral particles This strategy of hijacking the cell cycle to amplify infection is not unique to PRRSV; it has also been observed in various other viruses such as HIV and EV-D68, reflecting a common and widespread viral mechanism [22–26].

Beyond directly interfering with the cell cycle, PRRSV can also induce ER stress in MARC-145 cells and specifically activate the protein kinase RNA-like endoplasmic reticulum kinase (PERK) and inositol-requiring enzyme 1 (IRE1) signaling pathways of the unfolded protein response (UPR) (Figure 1) [27]. Within the PERK pathway, PRRSV promotes eukaryotic initiation factor 2 alpha (eIF2α) phosphorylation and the expression of downstream transcription factors activating transcription factor 4 (ATF4) and growth arrest and DNA damage-inducible protein 34 (GADD34), ultimately triggering caspase-3 and poly (ADP-ribose) polymerase (PARP) activation via the key effector C/EBP homologous protein (CHOP), thereby initiating ER stress-dependent apoptosis. Strategically, while PRRSV-induced ER stress promotes apoptosis, it also upregulates the conversion of LC3-II and the expression of Beclin1 to mediate autophagy [27]. This autophagic response can, to some extent, alleviate the pressure caused by ER stress (Figure 1). The regulation of apoptosis by PRRSV is not unidirectional promotion but rather a highly dynamic and temporally precise process. During the early stage of PRRSV infection, the virus suppresses apoptosis in MARC-145 cells by activating the phosphoinositide 3-kinase/protein kinase B (PI3K/Akt) and p53 signaling pathways and reducing PARP cleavage, thereby creating a favorable intracellular environment for viral genome replication and early protein synthesis [28]. Further studies indicate that the PRRSV GP2a protein can modulate the NF-κB/AP1 transcription factor pathway to delay host cell apoptosis (Figure 1). Concurrently, PRRSV infection activates the PI3K/Akt signaling pathway, leading to phosphorylation of the BCL2-associated agonist of cell death (BAD) protein. The phosphorylated BAD interacts with the autophagy protein Beclin1, an interaction identified as one of the key mechanisms by which PRRSV inhibits host cell apoptosis in the early infection stage [29]. Notably, this anti-apoptotic effect is weaker in porcine alveolar macrophages (PAMs) compared to MARC-145 cells, resulting in an earlier onset of apoptosis in PAMs [29]. In the late stage of infection, PRRSV strategically shifts toward promoting cell death by coordinately activating multiple apoptotic pathways through its non-structural proteins (NSPs) and structural proteins (Figure 1). For instance, PRRSV NSP4, relying on its 3C-like protease activity, cleaves the mitochondrial inner membrane protein cytochrome c1 (cyto c1) at the E230|G231 site. The resulting N-terminal fragment disrupts mitochondrial integrity and directly induces the collapse of the mitochondrial membrane potential [30]. Additionally, NSP4 can activate the mitochondrial apoptotic pathway by activating the pro-apoptotic protein Bim and inducing the degradation of the anti-apoptotic protein B-cell lymphoma-extra large (Bcl-xL) [28]. PRRSV NSP10 primarily relies on the activation of caspase-8 and upregulates the pro-apoptotic protein BH3 interacting-domain death agonist (Bid) and its active form, truncated Bid (tBid), thereby establishing a critical cross-talk between the mitochondrial pathway and the extrinsic apoptotic pathway [28]. On the other hand, the PRRSV structural protein E interacts with mitochondrial ATP5A and the ADP/ATP translocase, inhibiting cellular ATP production and thereby exacerbating the apoptotic process at the level of energy metabolism [31]. Ultimately, through these multi-faceted late-stage mechanisms, PRRSV coordinately activates the three core apoptotic pathways—the extrinsic death receptor pathway, the intrinsic mitochondrial pathway, and the ER stress pathway—as evidenced by the sequential activation of caspase-8, −9, and −12, collectively driving the cell toward apoptosis to maximize the efficient release of viral particles [30]. Figure 1.

sec.title: Host factors and therapeutic targeting
sec.p: Host factors also play critical roles in regulating PRRSV-induced apoptosis, beyond the direct actions of viral proteins (Figure 1). Research indicates that the macrophage receptor with collagenous structure (MARCO), a member of class A scavenger receptors, is specifically expressed in PAMs [32]. MARCO interacts with the major PRRSV envelope protein GP5 and promotes caspase-3 cleavage via its N-terminal intracellular domain, thereby exacerbating the apoptotic process during the middle to late stages of viral infection and ultimately restricting viral proliferation. PRRSV counteracts this host antiviral mechanism by downregulating MARCO expression [32]. Additionally, interferon-gamma-inducible protein 30 (IFI30), which plays an important role in antigen presentation, exhibits anti-apoptotic functions. In MARC-145 cells, overexpression of porcine IFI30 alleviates apoptosis and significantly suppresses viral replication. Conversely, knockdown of IFI30 in swine testicular cells enhances both apoptosis and viral replication, confirming its anti-apoptotic role [33].

Building on a deeper understanding of apoptosis regulation, researchers have begun exploring the therapeutic potential of using exogenous agents to modulate the apoptotic process and inhibit PRRSV replication (Figure 1). For instance, chitosan-coated selenium nanoparticles (CS-SeNPs) can mitigate PRRSV-induced oxidative stress, regulate the balance of the GSH-Px/GSH/H2O2 system, and inhibit the activation of the ROS/JNK signaling pathway and the subsequent cleavage of its downstream effectors caspase-3 and PARP. This collectively reduces apoptosis in MARC-145 cells and ultimately blocks viral replication [34]. In contrast, another compound, moroxydine hydrochloride (YASML), suppresses viral proliferation by activating the TLR3-dependent caspase-8/caspase-3 cascade and downregulating the anti-apoptotic protein Mcl-1 to enhance apoptosis [35]. Although these two strategies appear contradictory, they profoundly reflect the phase-dependent dual role of apoptosis during PRRSV infection. Compounds like CS-SeNPs primarily exert antioxidant and anti-apoptotic effects to protect cellular integrity and block the viral replication cycle. Conversely, pro-apoptotic compounds like YASML may preferentially eliminate virus-infected cells in the later stages of infection by activating the caspase cascade, thereby reducing the overall viral load. This suggests that adopting differential apoptosis modulation strategies tailored to distinct infection stages could be an important direction for future anti-PRRSV drug development.

In infection models, the PRRSV HuN4 strain alone can induce apoptosis via the caspase-3 pathway in peripheral immune organs such as the inguinal lymph nodes, accompanied by a reduction in lymphocyte count and impaired immune function. Furthermore, when a primary PRRSV HuN4 infection is followed by a secondary infection with Streptococcus suis strain BM0806, the co-infection synergistically enhances caspase-3-dependent apoptosis, significantly aggravating pathological damage in immune organs and the severity of clinical manifestations [36]. This phenomenon indicates that co-infections can accelerate disease progression by synergistically promoting apoptosis-mediated immunosuppression. Collectively, these findings reveal that host factors constitute a crucial line of defense against PRRSV, and their regulatory mechanisms provide new molecular targets for antiviral drug design. Exogenous compounds demonstrate potential therapeutic value through phase-dependent modulation of apoptosis, whereas excessive apoptosis induced by mixed infections is a key mechanism underlying clinical immunosuppression and exacerbated disease.

During early infection, PRRSV suppresses apoptosis via the PI3K/Akt pathway, which promotes BAD phosphorylation. In late infection, the virus shifts to promoting apoptosis through multiple mechanisms: non-structural protein NSP4 activates the mitochondrial pathway; and NSP10 links the mitochondrial and extrinsic apoptotic pathways. PRRSV also induces ER stress, activating the PERK/IRE1–CHOP—caspase-3 axis. Host factors (e.g. MARCO, IFI30) and exogenous compounds (e.g. CS-SeNPs, YASML) further modulate apoptotic signaling, illustrating the dynamic interplay between viral manipulation and host regulatory networks.

Symbol Definition:→ Direct activation (Red arrows indicate viral modulation; Black arrows indicate apoptosis pathway components); ⊣ Direct inhibition; ↔ Interaction;→ Phosphorylation modification; → Cleavage reaction; Green boxes: Exogenous agents/therapeutic compounds.

sec.title: Viral mechanisms for autophagy regulation
sec.p: Autophagy is an evolutionarily highly conserved intracellular degradation process, characterized by the formation of double-membraned autophagosomes and primarily regulated by core signaling pathways such as mechanistic target of rapamycin (mTOR) and AMP-activated protein kinase (AMPK) [37]. During PRRSV infection, the virus intervenes in autophagy-related signaling pathways and key molecules, establishing a complex regulatory network involving multiple factors to achieve immune evasion and promote its own replication [38]. In the early stage of infection (16–36 hpi), PRRSV upregulates the expression of mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue lymphoma translocation protein 1 (MALT1), thereby suppressing the inhibitory phosphorylation of mTOR and its downstream target unc-51 like autophagy activating kinase 1 (ULK1), and promoting the nuclear translocation of the transcription factor transcription factor EB to enhance lysosome biogenesis [39]. Concurrently, the virus inhibits the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), reduces lysosomal membrane permeability, and maintains lysosomal structural and functional integrity. Through the synergistic action of these dual mechanisms, the fusion of autophagosomes with lysosomes is effectively promoted, facilitating the smooth progression of the autophagic flux, thereby creating favorable conditions for the efficient release of viral particles and cell-to-cell transmission [40]. However, upon entering the late stage of infection, PRRSV-mediated MALT1 expression declines, weakening its promotive effect on autophagy. This leads to impaired autophagosome-lysosome fusion, resulting in the accumulation of numerous undegraded autophagosomes within the cell. These accumulated vesicles subsequently serve as storage compartments for viral particles, thereby promoting viral latency and persistence [39]. In other words, during the early infection stage, PRRSV promotes complete autophagic flux by enhancing autophagosome-lysosome fusion, whereas in the late stage, it restricts autophagy, leading to autophagosome accumulation and incomplete autophagy, thereby supporting efficient viral release and intracellular storage, respectively. MALT1, as a key host factor regulating autophagic flux, plays a central role in this dynamic process.

PRRSV further establishes a sophisticated regulatory network by directly targeting various stages of the autophagy machinery through multiple viral proteins (Figure 2). The virus induces ER-derived autophagy initiation; however, it subsequently utilizes its nonstructural proteins NSP3 and NSP5 to block autophagosome-lysosome fusion, thereby resulting in incomplete autophagy [40]. Furthermore, the viral NSP2 protein can colocalize with the autophagy marker protein LC3, exploiting incomplete autophagy to provide a favorable niche for viral replication [41]. Notably, inhibiting autophagy significantly reduces viral yield, whereas inhibiting apoptosis under autophagy-deficient conditions can restore viral replication [42], indicating an antagonistic relationship between autophagy and apoptosis during PRRSV infection. Further investigations have revealed that PRRSV modulates host lipid metabolic pathways to facilitate its own replication. Specifically, PRRSV-2 activates the retinoic acid-inducible gene I/mitochondrial antiviral-signaling protein/nuclear factor kappa B (RIG-I/MAVS/NF-κB) signaling pathway, leading to upregulation of the small GTPase RAS-related protein Rab-18. This subsequently promotes the degradation of the lipid droplet protective protein perilipin-2 via the chaperone-mediated autophagy (CMA) pathway, thereby enhancing lipolysis and supplying essential lipid resources for viral replication and assembly [43]. Moreover, PRRSV downregulates the expression of N-myc downstream regulated gene 1, which further activates autophagy and promotes lipid droplet degradation, thereby intensifying lipid mobilization [44]. Intracellular Ca2+ signaling pathways play a critical role in PRRSV-mediated regulation of autophagy. When Ca2+ is released from stores such as the ER into the cytosol, it can bind to and activate calmodulin, subsequently driving downstream effectors like the AMPK/mTORC1 axis or the ULK1/FIP200 complex, thereby initiating autophagy. In this process, the PRRSV NSP2 and GP5 proteins play central regulatory roles [45–47]. NSP2 interacts with the ER stress protein glucose-regulated protein 78 and the calcium sensor stromal interaction molecule 1 (STIM1), activating STIM1-Orai1-mediated store-operated calcium entry, facilitating the influx of extracellular Ca2+ into the ER. Subsequently, Ca2+ is released into the cytoplasm via inositol trisphosphate receptor (IP3R) channels, activating the CaMKII-AMPK-mTOR signaling axis to induce autophagy, thereby supporting PRRSV replication [48]. On the other hand, the GP5 protein enhances membrane contact sites between the ER and mitochondria, promoting the flow of Ca2+ from the ER to mitochondria via IP3R and voltage-dependent anion channel 1 (VDAC1) channels, causing mitochondrial Ca2+ overload. This process not only leads to mitochondrial dysfunction and accumulation of mitochondrial ROS but also activates autophagy via the AMPK/mTOR/ULK1 pathway. This activation subsequently inhibits NOD-like receptor family, pyrin domain containing 3 (NLRP3) inflammasome activation, dampens the host innate immune response, and promotes viral replication [49]. Another study found that PRRSV infection upregulates sigma non-opioid intracellular receptor 1 expression, promoting the formation of mitochondria-ER contact sites. This facilitates Ca2+ entry into mitochondria via the IP3R-VDAC1-mitochondrial calcium uniporter (MCU) channel, exacerbating mitochondrial Ca2+ overload. The Ca2+ overload, on one hand, activates the CaMKKβ-AMPK-dynamin-related protein 1 (DRP1) pathway to induce mitochondrial fission, and on the other hand, initiates mitophagy via the PTEN-induced putative kinase 1-Parkin RBR E3 ubiquitin-protein ligase (PINK1-Parkin)pathway. These two pathways collectively inhibit oxidative phosphorylation and enhance glycolysis, thereby remodeling host energy metabolism to provide metabolic support for PRRSV replication [50]. These findings collectively demonstrate that PRRSV employs a sophisticated strategy to spatiotemporally regulate multiple forms of autophagy and metabolic pathways, thereby shaping an intracellular environment that facilitates viral replication and dissemination. Figure 2.
Schematic diagram illustrating the mechanisms of PRRSV-mediated multidimensional regulation of autophagy.PRRSV NSP3 and NSP5 interact with ER stress and STM1-orail, increasing Ca2+ and activating AMPK. PRRSV NSP2 and GP5 influence Ca2+ flow via IP3R and VDAC1. The process involves phagophore, autophagosome, lysosome and autolysosome stages. PRRSV NSP2 interacts with GRASP65, RAB2 and SH3KBP1, affecting ULK1 and mTOR pathways. TRIM25 and P62 are involved in LC3-I to LC3-II conversion. PRRSV NSP12 interacts with NBR1 and PSMB1, affecting autophagy processes and colocalization with LC3.This schematic diagram illustrates the multifaceted mechanisms by which PRRSV regulates host autophagy through multiple signaling axes, including ER stress, calcium homeostasis, AMPK/mTOR, and autophagy receptor pathways, via distinct viral proteins.

sec.title: Autophagy-mediated immune evasion
sec.p: In addition to reshaping the intracellular environment via its own viral proteins to promote replication, PRRSV also achieves effective immune evasion by degrading key host antiviral factors (Figure 2). The E3 ubiquitin ligase tripartite motif-containing 25 (TRIM25) acts as a host restriction factor that suppresses PRRSV replication by inhibiting p62-mediated autophagy. Studies have found that PRRSV infection induces autophagic degradation of ubiquitinated protein aggregates, whereas TRIM25—through its E3 ubiquitin ligase activity—promotes K63-linked polyubiquitination of p62, thereby inhibiting its oligomerization and interaction with LC3 and ubiquitinated proteins. This results in blocked autophagic flux and restricted PRRSV replication [51]. PRRSV NSP2, in turn, exploits the autophagy pathway to degrade the host antiviral protein SH3 domain-containing kinase-binding protein 1 (SH3KBP1), thereby attenuating innate immune responses. SH3KBP1, an adaptor protein, recruits TRIM25 to enhance K63-linked polyubiquitination of RIG-I, promoting type I interferon signaling and suppressing PRRSV replication. To evade this mechanism, PRRSV NSP2 binds to SH3KBP1 via its amino acid motif 453–458, directing SH3KBP1 for degradation via the autophagic-lysosomal pathway and weakening the host antiviral response [52]. Thus, by mediating the autophagic degradation of SH3KBP1, PRRSV NSP2 precisely disrupts the upstream segment of the host viral RNA-sensing signaling pathway, representing an efficient strategy for immune evasion. Furthermore, NSP2 utilizes its proteolytic activity to degrade the Golgi structural protein Golgi reassembly stacking protein (GRASP65), leading to Golgi fragmentation. This causes RAB2 to dissociate from GM130 and subsequently bind the autophagy initiation protein ULK1, enhancing ULK1 phosphorylation, activating autophagy, and ultimately promoting viral replication [49]. Proteasome 20S subunit beta 1 (PSMB1), another host restriction factor, interacts with NSP12 and induces ubiquitination at lysine 130 of NSP12, thereby inhibiting viral replication. This process depends on the selective autophagy receptor neighbor of BRCA1 gene 1. Notably, PRRSV infection downregulates PSMB1 expression via the early B-cell factor 1, attenuating its antiviral function [53]. PRRSV immune evasion strategies are not limited to nonstructural proteins; structural proteins also play critical roles. The viral envelope protein GP5 competitively binds the Hinge domain of LAMP2A, the key receptor for CMA, disrupting its interaction with the CMA substrate-recognition chaperone HSC70 and thereby weakening host antiviral defense [54]. Moreover, GP5 inhibits the mTORC2/PHLPP1/GFAP pathway, interfering with the stability of the phosphorylated GFAP—EF1α complex. This leads to the formation of a stable complex between non-phosphorylated GFAP and LAMP2A, impairing CMA function [54]. GP5 also blocks K63-linked polyubiquitination of LAMP2A, further reducing its protein stability. Under normal conditions, CMA counteracts viral immune evasion by degrading PRRSV NSP11, which otherwise suppresses type I interferon signaling. Thus, GP5-mediated suppression of CMA alleviates this antagonistic effect, further enhancing viral immune evasion and creating a favorable environment for persistent infection [54].

PRRSV utilizes its non-structural (NSP2, NSP3, NSP5) and structural (GP5) proteins to spatiotemporally regulate autophagy by modulating host signaling pathways, organelle dynamics, and selective degradation. The virus manipulates ER stress and calcium signaling through NSP2 interactions with GRP78 and STIM1, activating the CaMKII-AMPK-mTOR axis to initiate autophagy. Concurrently, GP5 enhances ER-mitochondria contact sites to induce mitochondrial Ca2+ overload, further activating the AMPK/mTOR/ULK1 pathway. The virus promotes autophagosome formation while strategically blocking their fusion with lysosomes via NSP3 and NSP5, resulting in incomplete autophagy and accumulation of viral particles. Autophagy is further amplified through the NSP2-mediated “GRASP65-RAB2-ULK1” axis, wherein Golgi fragmentation releases RAB2 to bind and activate ULK1. PRRSV also exploits selective autophagy receptors (p62, LC3, NBR1) to degrade host antiviral factors such as SH3KBP1 and PSMB1, thereby evading innate immune responses. Collectively, these interconnected mechanisms enable PRRSV to remodel the host intracellular environment, promoting viral replication and persistence.

Symbol Definition:→ Direct activation (Red arrows indicate viral modulation; brown arrows indicate autophagy pathway components); ⊣ Direct inhibition; ↔ Functional interaction; → Degradation reaction; blue boxes: antiviral factors.

sec.title: Host countermeasures and therapeutic targeting
sec.p:
In response to PRRSV immune evasion strategies, the host has evolved a series of defense mechanisms (Figure 3), among which the DDX protein family serves as an important participant. The DDX protein family comprises RNA helicases containing a conserved Asp-Glu-Ala-Asp (DEAD) motif, which are widely involved in cellular RNA metabolism and play key regulatory roles in the host antiviral immune response [55]. Studies demonstrate that DDX27 specifically binds to the PRRSV structural protein GP2a and promotes its K48- and K63-linked polyubiquitination, leading to GP2a degradation via the p62-mediated selective autophagic-lysosomal pathway, thereby ultimately inhibiting viral assembly and replication. Furthermore, overexpression of DDX27 significantly increases the accumulation of the autophagic flux marker LC3-II and reduces p62 protein levels, indicating that DDX27 can activate cellular autophagy [56]. Another DDX family member, DDX10, also exhibits anti-PRRSV activity. DDX10 suppresses viral replication by positively regulating type I interferon (IFN-I) and downstream interferon-stimulated gene expression. Upon PRRSV infection, DDX10 translocates from the nucleus to the cytoplasm and is degraded via the autophagy pathway. The viral envelope protein E interacts with DDX10 and induces its degradation through a p62-dependent selective autophagy mechanism, thereby eliminating DDX10 to help the virus evade innate immune responses [57]. A solid line connects from the PRRSV icon toward a hexagon labeled IFN dash beta and a perpendicular blocking bar is drawn on that line. Another solid arrow extends from the IKK epsilon region toward the IFN dash beta label. The image A showing no graphs, no plotted data and no axes with labels or units.This schematic illustrates the crosstalk between host autophagy and PRRSV infection, showing host antiviral defenses and viral countermeasures targeting the autophagy-immune axis.

Beyond directly targeting host antiviral proteins, PRRSV also remodels the cellular defense landscape at the epitranscriptomic level to favor its replication. A key mechanism involves the upregulation and nucleocytoplasmic translocation of the methyltransferase-like 3 (METTL3) [58]. METTL3 exerts a dual pro-viral function: first, it mediates m6 A modification of the mRNA encoding the autophagy adaptor p62, enhancing p62 expression and autophagic activity; second, it interacts with and promotes the K63-linked ubiquitination and subsequent autophagic degradation of IKKε, a critical kinase in the interferon signaling pathway. This coordinated action—bolstering autophagy while dismantling a key component of IFN-β production—creates a cellular environment conducive to PRRSV replication [9]. This example underscores how PRRSV extends its manipulation beyond canonical signaling pathways to include RNA modification networks, all converging on the autophagy machinery to dampen antiviral immunity.

Given the central role of autophagy in PRRSV immune evasion, targeting this pathway has emerged as a promising therapeutic strategy. The natural flavonoid hyperoside has been found to exert antiviral effects by inhibiting PRRSV-induced autophagy. Hyperoside activates the p62/Nrf2/Keap1 signaling axis, promoting p62 accumulation and Nrf2 nuclear translocation, thereby suppressing the virus-induced conversion of LC3-I to LC3-II and autophagosome formation. This autophagy-inhibitory effect correlates closely with reduced viral replication, indicating that hyperoside effectively restricts PRRSV proliferation by modulating the autophagy pathway [9]. This studies DDX proteins, METTL3, and hyperoside collectively highlight p62 as a pivotal molecular hub at the intersection of host antiviral defense and PRRSV subversion. On one hand, p62 serves as an essential executor of intrinsic immunity, mediating the autophagic degradation of viral proteins (e.g. GP2a via DDX27) to restrict replication. On the other hand, PRRSV adeptly co-opts this very pathway, using p62 to eliminate host restriction factors (e.g. DDX10 and IKKε) and enhancing its expression via METTL3 to amplify autophagy. Consequently, therapeutic strategies, such as hyperoside, that precisely modulate the p62 pathway represent a promising avenue for tipping this balance in favor of the host and controlling PRRSV infection.

Host cells deploy autophagy-related mechanisms to restrict PRRSV infection, while the virus evolves strategies to subvert these defenses. The RNA helicase DDX27 binds PRRSV GP2a, promotes its K48/K63-linked polyubiquitination, and targets it for p62-mediated autophagic degradation, concurrently enhancing autophagic flux. In contrast, PRRSV envelope protein E binds and degrades the interferon-stimulatory helicase DDX10 via p62-dependent selective autophagy, blunting IFN-I responses. The virus further upregulates METTL3, which enhances p62 expression through m6 A mRNA modification to promote autophagy, while also mediating K63-linked ubiquitination and autophagic degradation of IKKε to suppress IFN-β production. The natural compound hyperoside inhibits PRRSV-induced autophagy by activating the p62/Nrf2/Keap1 axis, attenuating autophagosome formation and viral replication. This figure illustrates the dynamic interplay between host autophagy-mediated antiviral efforts and viral immune evasion tactics.
Symbol Definition:→ Direct activation; ⊣ Direct inhibition; ↔ Functional interaction; →Degradation reaction; pink boxes: host proteins.

Although host cells can degrade certain viral proteins (such as NSP12 and NSP1α) through autophagy, thereby partially restricting PRRSV replication, overall, PRRSV successfully hijacks the host autophagy system and transforms it into a powerful tool to facilitate viral infection. By leveraging viral proteins like NSP2 and GP5, the virus activates pathways such as ER stress, calcium signaling, mitochondrial ROS release, and Golgi fragmentation, thereby inducing various forms of autophagy and constructing an intracellular environment favorable for viral replication. Furthermore, PRRSV employs selective autophagy to degrade key host antiviral factors—including SH3KBP1, MDA5, DDX10, and ISG15—effectively suppressing the type I interferon signaling pathway and weakening the host’s innate immune response. In addition, the virus induces specialized types of autophagy such as lipophagy, aggrephagy, and mitophagy, which further remodel the intracellular metabolic landscape and delay apoptosis, thereby supporting persistent viral infection and replication.

sec.title: Modulation of other PCD pathways by PRRSV
Pyroptosis
sec.p: Pyroptosis is a form of PCD triggered by caspase-1 (activated by inflammasomes) or caspase-4/5/11-mediated cleavage of Gasdermin D (GSDMD). The resulting N-terminal fragment (GSDMD-N) forms pores in the cell membrane, leading to cellular swelling, membrane rupture, and the release of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-1β (IL-1β) and IL-18. This process plays a critical role in anti-infective immunity. Studies have shown that in weaned piglet ileum tissue, PRRSV infection activates the nuclear receptor NR4A1, which transcriptionally upregulates the NLRP3 inflammasome and pyroptosis-related genes (IL1β, IL18, ASC, GSDMD, CASP1), thereby inducing pyroptosis and ultimately leading to ileal inflammatory injury and impaired growth performance [59]. In a co-infection model using the PAM39 cell line, which supports simultaneous replication of PRRSV and classical swine fever virus (CSFV), it was found that GSDMD-dependent pyroptosis and IL-1β secretion induced by PRRSV strains JXwn06, HB-1/3.9, and CHsx1401 significantly suppressed the replication of the CSFV C-strain vaccine. Astragalus polysaccharide was shown to enhance CSFV C-strain replication by alleviating pyroptosis, providing a novel mechanistic insight into vaccine failure in cases of PRRSV co-infection [60]. Another study demonstrated that both in vitro infection of PAMs and Marc-145 cells with the J×A1 strain, and in vivo infection of piglets with the GDBY1 strain, led to the dispersal of the trans-Golgi network (TGN) from continuous tubular-vesicular structures into discrete vesicles induced by the PRRSV replication-transcription complex. The dispersed TGN acts as a scaffold by enriching phosphatidylinositol-4-phosphate, facilitating the assembly and activation of the NLRP3 inflammasome. This promotes caspase-1 activation and GSDMD cleavage, ultimately executing pyroptosis. It is noteworthy that neither PRRSV genomic RNA, viral RNA transcripts, nor the major structural proteins E, M, or N alone could induce pyroptosis. Furthermore, the NLRP3-specific inhibitor MCC950 and the caspase-1 inhibitor Z-YVAD-FMK blocked NLRP3 activation and GSDMD cleavage, respectively, confirming that this pyroptotic pathway depends on the NLRP3-caspase-1 axis [61].

sec.title: Ferroptosis
sec.p: Ferroptosis is an iron-dependent form of regulated cell death characterized by the accumulation of lipid peroxides. Its core mechanism involves the inactivation of glutathione peroxidase 4 (GPX4) and the collapse of cellular antioxidant defense systems. PRRSV co-opts the host protein DDX3X and triggers ferroptosis to facilitate viral replication. Studies indicate that PRRSV infection markedly upregulates DDX3X expression in a manner dependent on viral replication. Suppression of DDX3X via the inhibitor RK-33 or siRNA knockdown significantly reduced PRRSV replication, whereas overexpression of DDX3X enhanced viral propagation. Integrated transcriptomic and metabolomic analyses further demonstrated that DDX3X silencing perturbs key biological processes, including ferroptosis, the FoxO signaling pathway, and glutathione metabolism [62]. PRRSV infection induced characteristic morphological alterations associated with ferroptosis, such as mitochondrial condensation, loss of cristae, and outer membrane rupture, accompanied by decreased GPX4 levels and increased expression of ferroptosis-related genes, including NCOA4, ACSL4, and STEAP3. These changes resulted in intracellular Fe2+ accumulation and elevated lipid peroxidation. Furthermore, pharmacological induction of ferroptosis promoted PRRSV replication, whereas its inhibition attenuated viral production. Collectively, these findings reveal that PRRSV enhances its replication by modulating the DDX3X—ferroptosis axis, indicating that targeting ferroptosis may offer a novel therapeutic strategy for controlling PRRSV infection [62].

sec.title: Integrated modulation of PCD networks during PRRSV infection
sec.p: The modulation of host PCD pathways by PRRSV is not an isolated process. Instead, through shared signaling hubs and metabolic nodes, the virus orchestrates a highly dynamic and coordinated regulatory network for cell death. This network enables PRRSV to spatiotemporally fine-tune the balance between different PCD modalities, thereby optimizing its replication niche, facilitating immune evasion, and sustaining persistent infection.

sec.title: Multifaceted interplay of PCD pathways
sec.p: During PRRSV infection, apoptosis and autophagy exhibit a complex, context-dependent relationship. In the early stage, PRRSV suppresses apoptosis via signaling pathways such as PI3K/Akt while promoting a complete autophagic flux to sustain cell viability, thus supporting viral replication. At this phase, autophagy alleviates ER stress and mitochondrial dysfunction by clearing damaged organelles and misfolded proteins, indirectly delaying the initiation of apoptosis. This regulatory pattern demonstrates the virus’s capability to precisely balance apoptosis and autophagy. For instance, PRRSV-induced ER stress concurrently activates the pro-apoptotic CHOP pathway and the autophagy-associated conversion of LC3-I to LC3-II [27]. Autophagy may transiently mitigate cellular stress, postponing apoptosis until viral assembly is complete. As infection progresses to later stages, PRRSV shifts toward promoting multiple death programs. Viral non-structural proteins such as NSP4 and NSP10 activate both the mitochondrial and death receptor pathways [28,29]. These actions synergize with structural proteins like the E protein, which induces mitochondrial functional collapse [31], collectively facilitating the execution of apoptosis. Concurrently, the virus causes autophagic flux blockade by impairing autophagosome-lysosome fusion [40]. The accumulated autophagosomes not only serve as storage compartments for viral particles but also exacerbate cellular metabolic stress, further propelling the apoptotic process. At the molecular level, cross-talk between apoptosis and autophagy is mediated through shared regulators, such as the interaction between BAD and Beclin1 [29], reflecting the deep integration of these signaling pathways.

Autophagy during PRRSV infection is also intricately linked with the inflammatory cell death pathway, pyroptosis. Under physiological conditions, autophagy maintains homeostasis and exerts anti-inflammatory effects by clearing damage-associated molecular patterns. PRRSV subverts this function by disrupting autophagic flux. Virus-induced mitochondrial calcium overload, ROS accumulation, and Golgi apparatus fragmentation not only trigger autophagy but also provide a platform for the assembly of the NLRP3 inflammasome [49,50,61]. When autophagic degradation is impaired, uncleared organelle debris and oxidative products persistently activate NLRP3, thereby driving caspase-1-mediated cleavage of Gasdermin D and the onset of pyroptosis. Thus, while autophagic degradation could potentially restrict inflammasome activation, PRRSV’s inhibition of complete autophagy paradoxically promotes the accumulation of inflammasome activators, driving pyroptosis [61]. This shift from dysregulated autophagy to pyroptosis allows the virus to release progeny virions via cell lysis and modulate the local immune milieu through the released inflammatory factors.

Ferroptosis, an iron-dependent form of cell death driven by lipid peroxidation, intersects with other PCD pathways through shared metabolic and oxidative stress signals. The metabolic reprogramming and oxidative stress triggered by PRRSV infection converge extensively with ferroptosis, apoptosis, and autophagy. The virus induces mitochondrial dysfunction via its structural protein E and calcium overload [31,49], which not only triggers apoptosis and mitophagy but also directly promotes ferroptosis by increasing ROS production and inhibiting GPX4 activity [61]. Simultaneously, virus-mediated upregulation of DDX3X further disrupts glutathione metabolism—a core antioxidant pathway that also regulates apoptosis and autophagy. Furthermore, virus-activated selective autophagy forms, such as lipophagy (degradation of lipid droplets) [44], increase the pool of free fatty acids, providing more substrates for lipid peroxidation and thus synergistically enhancing ferroptosis at the metabolic level. Consequently, PRRSV’s activation of lipophagy and aggrephagy may cooperate with its induction of ferroptosis to collectively reshape cellular metabolism in support of viral replication, while inducing a unique death modality favorable for viral release and immune modulation.

sec.title: Spatiotemporal and cell-specific regulation
sec.p:
The modulation of the PCD network by PRRSV exhibits distinct spatiotemporal specificity and cell-type dependency, which constitutes a central strategy for its efficient infection (Table 1). In the temporal dimension, during the early phase of infection, the virus safeguards host cell survival by inhibiting apoptosis and maintaining a complete autophagic flux [29,39], thereby creating a replication window. As the infection progresses to later stages, the strategy shifts toward promoting apoptosis [28,29], blocking autophagic flux [40], and activating inflammatory death pathways such as pyroptosis [60,61], thereby facilitating the release and spread of progeny virions. In the cell-type dimension, the regulation exhibits a high degree of specificity. For instance, within primary target cells like PAMs, the virus favors the inhibition of apoptosis in the early stage to prolong the replication cycle. Conversely, in certain immune cells, it may promote apoptosis to dampen the host’s adaptive immune response, thereby achieving immune evasion.Table 1.
Spatiotemporal regulation strategies of PCD by PRRSV.
PCDEarly infectionLate infectionCell type differences
ApoptosisSuppression (e.g. via PI3K/Akt activation, BAD phosphorylation & Beclin1 interaction) [29]Promotion (e.g. via NSP4-mediated mitochondrial activation [28], NSP10-mediated extrinsic pathway linkage [28], & structural protein E-induced mitochondrial dysfunction [31])Strong anti-apoptotic effect in MARC-145 cells; earlier apoptosis onset in PAMs.
AutophagyPromotion of complete flux (e.g. via MALT1 upregulation enhancing autophagosome-lysosome fusion) [39]Induction of flux blockade (e.g. via NSP3/NSP5-blocked fusion leading to autophagosome accumulation & viral “reservoir” formation) [40]Intensity and mechanisms may vary by cell type (e.g. epithelial cells vs. macrophages).
PyroptosisLikely suppressed to avoid premature inflammationActive induction (e.g. via Golgi disruption providing a scaffold for NLRP3 inflammasome assembly) [60,61]Particularly prominent in immune cells (e.g. PAMs), directly linked to immunomodulation and pathological damage.
FerroptosisMechanism to be clarified (may relate to early metabolic adaptation)Induction (e.g. via DDX3X upregulation, glutathione metabolism disruption, GPX4 inhibition) [62]Likely closely associated with cellular metabolic status; more readily triggered in metabolically active cells.
Core ObjectiveMaintain cell viability, establish viral replication factoriesPromote virion release, evade immune clearance, and modulate microenvironment via inflammationFine-tuning viral strategy per host cell type’s microenvironment and function.

This multi-layered and differential fine-tuning relies on the multifunctional synergy of various viral proteins. Acting as critical nodes, these proteins simultaneously intervene in multiple hosts signaling pathways. For example, NSP2 participates in autophagosome formation and can degrade host adapter proteins to interfere with innate immunity [52]. GP5 inhibits chaperone-mediated autophagy and modulates calcium signaling, thereby affecting mitochondrial function [54]. The E protein directly targets mitochondrial energy metabolism, coordinately regulating apoptosis and cell survival [31]. By acting on key cellular processes—including calcium signaling, ER stress, mitochondrial integrity, and ubiquitination—PRRSV employs its limited genetic repertoire to construct a multi-target, multi-pathway PCD regulatory network. This enables the virus to flexibly balance cell survival and death across different infection stages and diverse cellular environments, ultimately optimizing its own replication and dissemination efficiency.

sec.title: Therapeutic implications and network-based strategies
sec.p: From a therapeutic perspective, the PCD regulatory network orchestrated by PRRSV is characterized by high redundancy and compensatory capacity, with extensive crosstalk existing between different PCD pathways [38]. Consequently, targeting a single pathway in isolation is often of limited efficacy and may even trigger the compensatory activation of alternative death mechanisms, a phenomenon particularly evident in the autophagy-apoptosis balance [42]. Therefore, future antiviral strategies should focus on key hubs within this network. Examples include modulating calcium signaling homeostasis [48], targeting ROS metabolic balance, or intervening with core adaptor proteins like p62/SQSTM1 that integrate signals from autophagy, apoptosis, and inflammation [9,46,51]. A more optimal approach would be to develop spatiotemporally specific combination therapies. For instance, supporting the virus-clearing function of autophagy during early infection while inhibiting excessive inflammatory death or metabolic dysregulation in later stages [56,59,61]. A deeper understanding of the mechanisms by which PRRSV integrates host PCD networks not only reveals the complexity of viral pathogenesis but also provides a crucial theoretical foundation and direction for developing novel, broad-spectrum antiviral therapies capable of effectively dismantling the viral defense network.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: By intricately manipulating the host PCD network, PRRSV creates a favorable environment for its replication, immune evasion, and persistence. This review has systematically elucidated the specific molecular mechanisms by which PRRSV intervenes in various PCD modalities—apoptosis, autophagy, pyroptosis, and ferroptosis—revealing a complex regulatory system characterized by multi-viral protein synergy, spatiotemporal specificity, and cell type dependency. These mechanisms do not operate in isolation; rather, they are tightly interwoven through shared signaling hubs and metabolic nodes, forming a dynamically balanced death-regulatory network.

Looking ahead, future research in this field must move beyond describing single pathways to address more integrative and challenging scientific questions. First, there is a need to precisely decipher the molecular switches underlying the spatiotemporal and differential regulation of PCD by PRRSV, identifying the critical viral and host factors that determine the shift from a “pro-survival” to a “pro-death” state. Second, it is essential to clarify the quantitative relationships and functional outputs of the dynamic interactions between different PCD pathways, understanding how the virus converts this “crosstalk” into a replicative advantage. Finally, and most translationally significant, is the evaluation of novel intervention strategies targeting key nodes within the PCD network. Given the network’s redundancy, future antiviral approaches should favor the design of combinatorial strategies targeting multiple nodes, or the development of smart therapeutic strategies capable of spatiotemporally reshaping the PCD balance—for instance, by supporting antiviral autophagy in early infection while inhibiting detrimental inflammatory death in later stages.

In summary, in-depth investigation of the PRRSV-PCD network interplay will not only deepen our understanding of the pathogenesis of arteriviruses and other complex viruses but, more importantly, establish a solid theoretical foundation and provide a clear roadmap for developing next-generation broad-spectrum antiviral drugs and immunomodulatory strategies capable of dismantling the viral defense network. Future studies will need to integrate in vivo models, multi-omics approaches, and novel technologies to address these pivotal questions, ultimately bridging the gap from fundamental knowledge to clinical control.

sec.p: The authors declare that this study was conducted without any commercial or financial relationships that could be interpreted as potential conflicts of interest.
